target.name: Portimine

structural_features.ring_systems: 5-membered cyclic imine, cyclohexene, 14-membered macrocycle, medium-sized cyclic acetal

target.motivation: Portimine exhibits potent anticancer, antifouling, and anti-HIV-1 activities, selectively induces apoptosis in human cancer cell lines, and shows low acute toxicity in mice, making it a significant pharmaceutical lead compound. Its unique chemical structure also makes it an attractive synthetic target.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Catalytic asymmetric ene reaction for alpha-hydroxy 1,4-diketones
Asymmetric organocatalysis for rapid assembly of precursor chains

success_criteria.free_text: Synthesis of the macrocyclic core skeleton of portimine (compound 35) with correct stereochemistry, enabling late-stage oxidation and cyclic imine formation to yield portimine (1).

key_disconnections.named_strategy: Ring-closing metathesis (RCM) and transannular acetal formation
key_disconnections.reasoning: To construct the 14-membered macrocyclic core and the medium-sized cyclic acetal, leading to macrocyclic compound 2 from compound 3.
To convergently couple chiral alpha-alkoxyalkylstannane 4 and thioester 5, with high stereochemical retention at C-5.
To synthesize chiral alpha-alkoxyalkylstannane 4 from aldehyde 6.
To synthesize thioester 5 from readily available starting materials 7 and 8, controlling the configuration at C-3 and C-16.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The retrosynthetic strategy focuses on a convergent approach, utilizing a stereoretentive copper-catalyzed C(sp3)-C(sp2) Stille-type cross-coupling as a key step to assemble functionalized fragments. The macrocyclic core is formed via RCM and transannular acetal formation. Stereocontrol is established early through organocatalyst-mediated asymmetric stannylation and an asymmetric Diels-Alder reaction.

thought.background: The Evans syn-aldol reaction is a known method for stereoselective aldol condensations.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize a precursor for the alpha-alkoxyalkylstannane fragment.
thought.decision: To synthesize alcohol 11, chose the Evans syn-aldol reaction of aldehyde 9 and N-acyloxazolidinone 10, expecting high yield and excellent diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The Evans syn-aldol reaction proceeded smoothly to give alcohol 11 in 87% yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1).

evidence.text_span: The Evans syn-aldol reaction17 of aldehyde 918 and N-acyloxazolidinone 1019 proceeded smoothly to give alcohol 11 in 87% yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1).
evidence.section: Scheme 1. Synthesis of α-Alkoxyalkylstannane 15

thought.background: Alcohol 11 needs further transformation to introduce a leaving group and then a cyano group.
thought.gap: Need to convert the aldol product into a tosylate for subsequent cyanation.
thought.decision: To prepare alcohol 12, chose reduction of 11 using LiBH4 followed by tosylation, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Reduction of 11 using LiBH4 and subsequent tosylation gave alcohol 12 in 90% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Reduction of 11 using LiBH4 and subsequent tosylation gave alcohol 12 in 90% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Scheme 1. Synthesis of α-Alkoxyalkylstannane 15

thought.background: Alcohol 12 needs protection, cyanation, reduction, and methylenation to form the desired alkene.
thought.gap: Need to introduce the alkene functionality and protect the secondary alcohol.
thought.decision: To synthesize compound 13, chose protection of the secondary alcohol of 12 as a TIPS ether, followed by NaCN treatment, DIBALH reduction, and Wittig olefination, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Methylenation of the resulting aldehyde under Wittig olefination conditions provided compound 13 in 72% yield over four steps.

evidence.text_span: After protection of the secondary alcohol of 12 as a diisopropylsilyl (TIPS) ether, the cyano group was introduced by treatment with NaCN, and then reduced with diisobutylaluminum hydride (DIBALH) at −78 °C. Methylenation of the resulting aldehyde under Wittig olefination conditions provided compound 13 in 72% yield over four steps.
evidence.section: Scheme 1. Synthesis of α-Alkoxyalkylstannane 15

thought.background: Compound 13 contains a TBS group that needs selective removal.
thought.gap: Need to selectively deprotect the TBS group to reveal a primary alcohol.
thought.decision: To obtain the primary alcohol, chose treatment of compound 13 with NaOH at 60 °C, expecting smooth and selective removal of the TBS group.

outcome.observation: Treatment of compound 13 with NaOH at 60 °C smoothly and selectively removed the tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) group, and the corresponding primary alcohol was obtained in 99% yield.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of compound 13 with NaOH at 60 °C smoothly and selectively removed the tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) group, and the corresponding primary alcohol was obtained in 99% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 1. Synthesis of α-Alkoxyalkylstannane 15

thought.background: The primary alcohol needs to be oxidized to an aldehyde for the subsequent asymmetric stannylation.
thought.gap: Need to convert the primary alcohol to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To obtain aldehyde 14, chose Parikh-Doering oxidation of the primary alcohol, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Parikh-Doering oxidation of this alcohol provided aldehyde 14 in 94% yield.

evidence.text_span: Parikh-Doering oxidation (SO3·pyridine, Et3N, CH2Cl2 DMSO)20 of this alcohol provided aldehyde 14 in 94% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 1. Synthesis of α-Alkoxyalkylstannane 15

thought.background: Asymmetric stannylation is a key step to introduce the chiral stannane moiety.
thought.gap: Need to perform asymmetric stannylation on aldehyde 14 and protect the resulting alcohol.
thought.decision: To synthesize thiocarbamate 15, chose organocatalyst-mediated asymmetric stannylation of aldehyde 14 with (R)-diphenylprolinol, n-butyltin hydride, and diethylzinc, followed by immediate conversion of the unstable alcohol to thiocarbamate using a two-step protection sequence, expecting excellent diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Asymmetric stannylation with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr 97:3) was achieved. The obtained alcohol was unstable and immediately converted to thiocarbamate 15.

evidence.text_span: Thus, in accordance with Falck’s report, treatment of aldehyde 14 with (R)-diphenylprolinol in the presence of n-butyltin hydride and diethylzinc led to asymmetric stannylation with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr 97:3). However, the obtained alcohol was unstable and thus was immediately converted to thiocarbamate 15 using a two-step protection sequence.
evidence.section: Scheme 1. Synthesis of α-Alkoxyalkylstannane 15

thought.background: The asymmetric Diels-Alder reaction is a key step for constructing the cyclohexene ring with controlled stereochemistry.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize the spirocyclic cyclohexene 17 with high diastereo- and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To obtain spirocyclic cyclohexene 17, chose the asymmetric Diels-Alder reaction of alpha-methylene gamma-lactam 7 and diene 16 under Evans reaction conditions, expecting excellent selectivity.

outcome.observation: Asymmetric Diels-Alder reaction proceeded smoothly to give spirocyclic cyclohexene 17 in 89% yield with excellent diastereo and enantioselectivity (dr > 20:1 and er 98:2).

evidence.text_span: Asymmetric Diels−Alder reaction of α-methylene γ-lactam 723 α 723 and diene 1623 proceeded smoothly under Evans reaction conditions16 in the presence of [Cu((S,S) –tert–Bu-box)]-(SbF6)2 to give spirocyclic cyclohexene 17 in 89% yield with excellent diastereo and enantioselectivity (dr > 20:1 and er 98:2).
evidence.section: The synthesis of the thioester is shown in Scheme 2.

thought.background: The Cbz group needs to be removed and replaced with an Ns group.
thought.gap: Need to convert the Cbz-protected lactam 17 to the Ns-protected lactam 18.
thought.decision: To obtain Ns lactam 18, chose removal of the Cbz group using Et2AlCl and PhSMe, followed by introduction of a 2-nitrobenzenesulfonyl (Ns) group using n-BuLi and NsCl, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Removal of the Cbz group gave the corresponding N-H lactam in quantitative yield, and subsequent introduction of an Ns group gave Ns lactam 18 in 90% yield.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the Cbz group using Et2AlCl and PhSMe27 gave the corresponding N−H lactam in quantitative yield, and subsequent introduction of a 2-nitrobenzenesulfonyl (Ns) group by using n-BuLi and NsCl gave Ns lactam 18 in 90% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Synthesis of Thioester 21

thought.background: The lactam ring needs to be opened to reveal a carboxylic acid.
thought.gap: Need to convert Ns lactam 18 to carboxylic acid 19.
thought.decision: To obtain carboxylic acid 19, chose ring-opening of the lactam using LiOH, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Ring-opening of the lactam was carried out using LiOH, and carboxylic acid 19 was obtained in 86% yield.

evidence.text_span: Ring-opening of the lactam was carried out using LiOH, and carboxylic acid 19 was obtained in 86% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Synthesis of Thioester 21

thought.background: The carboxylic acid 19 needs to be converted to a thioester.
thought.gap: Direct introduction of thioesters to 19 is problematic due to lactam re-formation.
thought.decision: Attempted various methods to introduce thioesters by activating the carboxyl group of 19, expecting direct conversion.

outcome.observation: Various methods to introduce thioesters were unsuccessful; when the carboxyl group in 19 was activated, lactam 18 was easily formed by the nucleophilic attack of Ns amide.

evidence.text_span: Various methods to introduce thioesters were unsuccessful (i.e., when the carboxyl group in 19 was activated, lactam 18 was easily formed by the nucleophilic attack of Ns amide).
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Synthesis of Thioester 21

thought.background: Direct thioester formation from 19 failed due to lactam re-formation.
thought.gap: Need to prevent lactam re-formation during thioester introduction and protect the Ns amide.
thought.decision: To obtain carboxylic acid 20, chose to mask the carboxyl group in situ by treating 19 with TMS-imidazole, then protect the Ns amide with Boc under standard conditions, expecting to overcome the lactam re-formation issue.

outcome.observation: The problem was addressed by masking the carboxyl group in situ by treating 19 with TMS-imidazole. Next, the Ns amide was protected with tert-butoxycarbonyl (Boc) under standard reaction conditions to obtain carboxylic acid 20 in 80% yield in a one-pot operation.

evidence.text_span: This problem was addressed by masking the carboxyl group in situ by treating 19 with TMS-imidazole. Next, the Ns amide was protected with tert-butoxycarbonyl (Boc) under standard reaction conditions (Boc2O, Et3N, DMAP) to obtain carboxylic acid 20 in 80% yield in a one-pot operation.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Synthesis of Thioester 21

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 20 needs to be converted to the thioester 21.
thought.gap: Need to introduce the 4-nitrothiophenol moiety.
thought.decision: To synthesize thioester 21, chose conversion of 20 into the corresponding acid chloride, then treatment with 4-nitrothiophenol and pyridine, expecting to complete the synthesis.

outcome.observation: 20 was converted into the corresponding acid chloride, then treatment with 4-nitrothiophenol and pyridine completed the synthesis of thioester 21.

evidence.text_span: Finally, 20 was converted into the corresponding acid chloride, then treatment with 4-nitrothiophenol and pyridine completed the synthesis of thioester 21.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Synthesis of Thioester 21

thought.background: The key copper-catalyzed C(sp3)-C(sp2) Stille-type cross-coupling is crucial for convergent fragment assembly.
thought.gap: Need to couple stannane 15 and thioester 21 and reintroduce the Boc group.
thought.decision: To obtain coupling product 22, chose copper(I) thiophene-2-carboxylate (CuTC) as catalyst and heating in toluene, followed by reintroduction of the Boc group, expecting good yield and excellent diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The coupling reaction proceeded very slowly, taking 5 days to reach 48% yield. Further extension of reaction time or addition of catalysts resulted in lower yields. The reaction was carried out in a short time, and the resulting Bu3SnS·p·NO2Ph was removed. Then the same reaction was repeated three times and the product was pooled to obtain the total yield. The coupling product 22 was obtained in 61% overall yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1).

evidence.text_span: Investigation of the reaction conditions pointed to the use of copper(I) thiophene-2-carboxylate (CuTC)14b as a catalyst and heating in toluene. This removed the Boc group and thus we reintroduced the Boc group after the coupling reaction to afford the coupling product 22 in 61% overall yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1). However, the coupling reaction proceeded very slowly, taking 5 days to reach 48% yield. Further extension of reaction time or addition of catalysts resulted in lower yields. This was attributed to the byproduct Bu3SnS·p·NO2Ph 3 2produced during the reaction process,14b which was coordinated to the copper catalyst and inhibited cross-coupling. Therefore, the reaction was carried out in a short time, and the resulting Bu3SnS·p·NO2Ph was removed. Then the same reaction was repeated three times and the product was pooled to obtain the total yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 3. Key Coupling and Stereochemical Conformation of Alcohol 24 through Modified Mosher Analysis

thought.background: The thiocarbamate group needs to be selectively removed and the resulting intermediate converted to an alcohol for stereochemical confirmation.
thought.gap: Need to convert 22 to alcohol 24 for Mosher analysis.
thought.decision: To obtain alcohol 24, chose selective removal of thiocarbamate from 22 using m-CPBA to give formyl ester 23, followed by treatment with K2CO3 in MeOH, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Exposure of 22 to m-CPBA resulted in the selective removal of thiocarbamate to give formyl ester 23. Subsequent treatment of 23 with K2CO3 in MeOH provided alcohol 24 in 78% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Exposure of 22 to m-CPBA resulted in the selective removal of thiocarbamate28 to give formyl ester 23. Subsequent treatment of 23 with K2CO3 in MeOH provided alcohol 24 in 78% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Scheme 3. Key Coupling and Stereochemical Conformation of Alcohol 24 through Modified Mosher Analysis

thought.background: The stereochemistry of the newly formed C-5 position in 22 needs to be confirmed.
thought.gap: Need to confirm the configuration of the stereogenic center at the C-5 position.
thought.decision: To confirm the configuration, chose to convert alcohol 24 to (R)- and (S)-MTPA esters and analyze their configurations by a modified Mosher analysis, expecting to establish the stereochemistry.

outcome.observation: The configuration of the stereogenic center at the C-5 position in compound 22 was confirmed by a modified Mosher analysis. The key coupling reaction proceeded with extremely high stereochemical retention, and the chirality of alpha-alkoxyalkylstannane 15 was completely transferred to the coupling product 22.

evidence.text_span: We confirmed the configuration of the stereogenic center at the C-5 position in compound 22 as follows. Exposure of 22 to m-CPBA resulted in the selective removal of thiocarbamate28 to give formyl ester 23. Subsequent treatment of 23 with K2CO3 in MeOH provided alcohol 24 in 78% yield over two steps. Alcohol 24 was converted to (R)- and (S)-MTPA esters and their configurations were confirmed by a modified Mosher analysis,22 as shown in Scheme 3 (see Supporting Information for details). The key coupling reaction proceeded with extremely high stereochemical retention, and the chirality of α-alkoxyalkylstannane 15 was completely transferred to the coupling product 22.
evidence.section: Scheme 3. Key Coupling and Stereochemical Conformation of Alcohol 24 through Modified Mosher Analysis

thought.background: The TBDPS group needs to be selectively removed to enable further functionalization.
thought.gap: Need to selectively deprotect the TBDPS group to obtain primary alcohol 26.
thought.decision: To obtain primary alcohol 26, chose selective removal of the TBDPS group from 22 using a mixture of TBAF/AcOH, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: The tert-butyldiphenylsilyl (TBDPS) group was selectively removed using a mixture of TBAF/AcOH to obtain primary alcohol 26 in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: The tert-butyldiphenylsilyl (TBDPS) group was selectively removed using a mixture of TBAF/AcOH to obtain primary alcohol 26 in 91% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Construction of 14-Membered Macrocycles by RCM

thought.background: The primary alcohol 26 needs to be converted to an aldehyde and then reacted with a Grignard reagent to introduce the propargyl alcohol moiety.
thought.gap: Need to perform Swern oxidation and a Grignard reaction in a one-pot operation due to aldehyde instability.
thought.decision: To obtain propargyl alcohol 27, chose Swern oxidation of alcohol 26 followed immediately by a Grignard reaction in a one-pot operation, expecting good overall yield and excellent diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Swern oxidation of alcohol 26 proceeded smoothly, but the corresponding aldehyde was very unstable and decomposed during workup and purification. Therefore, the subsequent Grignard reaction was carried out immediately in a one-pot operation after the Swern oxidation step to obtain propargyl alcohol 27 in 98% overall yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1).

evidence.text_span: Swern oxidation of alcohol 26 ((COCl)2, Et3N, DMSO)30 proceeded smoothly, but the corresponding aldehyde was very unstable and decomposed during workup and purification. Therefore, the subsequent Grignard reaction was carried out immediately in a one-pot operation after the Swern oxidation step to obtain propargyl alcohol 27 in 98% overall yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1).
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Construction of 14-Membered Macrocycles by RCM

thought.background: The propargyl alcohol 27 needs to be converted to an alkene for RCM.
thought.gap: Need to introduce a vinyl group instead of an ethynyl group.
thought.decision: Attempted to introduce a vinyl group to 27, expecting a similar reactivity to the ethynyl group.

outcome.observation: Attempts to introduce functional groups other than ethynyl were unsuccessful, as the reaction was surprisingly very sluggish, in contrast to the introduction of the ethynyl group.

evidence.text_span: We also attempted to introduce vinyl group but the reaction was surprisingly very sluggish, in contrast to the introduction of ethynyl group, and thus our attempts to introduce functional groups other than ethynyl were unsuccessful.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Construction of 14-Membered Macrocycles by RCM

thought.background: Direct introduction of a vinyl group failed. The Ns group needs to be removed, the alkyne partially hydrogenated, and the allylic alcohol oxidized to an enone for RCM.
thought.gap: Need to prepare enone 28 from propargyl alcohol 27.
thought.decision: To obtain enone 28, chose removal of the Ns group with PhSH and K2CO3, partial hydrogenation of the terminal alkyne using Pd/CaCO3 and 3,6-dithia-1,8-octanediol (A), and PDC oxidation of the allylic alcohol, expecting to prepare the RCM substrate.

outcome.observation: Removal of the Ns group with PhSH, K2CO3, in DMF provided the corresponding amide in 91% yield. The terminal alkyne was partially hydrogenated to afford the corresponding allylic alcohol in quantitative yield. Furthermore, PDC oxidation of allylic alcohol gave enone 28 in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the Ns group with PhSH, K2CO3, in DMF provided the corresponding amide in 91% yield. The terminal alkyne (at C-14) was partially hydrogenated by using Pd/CaCO3, 3,6-dithia-1,8-octanediol (A)31 under a H2 atmosphere to afford the corresponding allylic alcohol in quantitative yield. Furthermore, PDC oxidation of allylic alcohol (at C-15) gave enone 28 in 88% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Construction of 14-Membered Macrocycles by RCM

thought.background: The RCM reaction is a key step for forming the 14-membered macrocycle.
thought.gap: Need to cyclize enone 28 to form the macrocyclic compound.
thought.decision: To obtain 14-membered macrocyclic compound 29, chose RCM reaction using the second Hoveyda-Grubbs catalyst (HG-II) in toluene, expecting excellent yield and good E/Z selectivity.

outcome.observation: RCM reaction using the second Hoveyda-Grubbs catalyst (HG-II, 21 mol %) in toluene (1.0 mM) provided 14-membered macrocyclic compound 29 in excellent yield (82%, E/Z = 5.8/1).

evidence.text_span: RCM reaction using the second Hoveyda-Grubbs catalyst (HG-II, 21 mol %)13 in toluene (1.0 mM) provided 14-membered macrocyclic compound 29 in excellent yield (82%, E/Z = 5.8/1).
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Construction of 14-Membered Macrocycles by RCM

thought.background: The 14-membered macrocycle 29-(E) needs to be converted to a diol for transannular acetal formation.
thought.gap: Need to reduce the ketone and deprotect the TIPS group to obtain diol 30.
thought.decision: To obtain diol 30, chose Luche reduction of 29-(E) followed by removal of the TIPS group using HF·pyridine, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Luche reduction of 29-(E) proceeded regioselectively at the C-15 ketone to give the desired allylic alcohol in 73% yield, along with its diastereomer in 18% yield. Removal of the TIPS group using HF·pyridine gave diol 30 in 92% yield.

evidence.text_span: Luche reduction (NaBH4, CeCl3·7H2O, EtOH, −78 to −60 °C)32 of 29-(E) proceeded regioselectively at the C-15 ketone to give the desired allylic alcohol in 73% yield, along with its diastereomer in 18% yield. Removal of the TIPS group using HF·pyridine in THF/pyridine (1:1) gave diol 30 in 92% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Challenges of Transannular Acetal Formation and Construction of the Macrocyclic Acetal Skeleton of Portimine

thought.background: Transannular acetal formation is a key step to construct the portimine core skeleton.
thought.gap: Need to form the acetal from diol 30.
thought.decision: Attempted various reaction conditions for transannular acetal formation using diol 30, expecting to obtain acetal 31.

outcome.observation: We investigated many reaction conditions for transannular acetal formation using diol 30 but were unable to obtain the desired acetal 31, possibly because distortion of acetal 31 by E-olefin made the cyclization process unfavorable.

evidence.text_span: We investigated many reaction conditions for transannular acetal formation using diol 30 but were unable to obtain the desired acetal 31, possibly because distortion of acetal 31 by E-olefin made the cyclization process unfavorable.
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Challenges of Transannular Acetal Formation and Construction of the Macrocyclic Acetal Skeleton of Portimine

thought.background: Transannular acetal formation from 29-(E) failed due to unfavorable distortion. The Z-isomer 29-(Z) might be more suitable.
thought.gap: Need to attempt transannular acetal formation starting from 29-(Z).
thought.decision: To obtain diol 32, chose Luche reduction of 29-(Z) followed by deprotection of the TIPS group, expecting excellent regio- and diastereoselectivity for the reduction and successful formation of the diol.

outcome.observation: Luche reduction of 29-(Z) proceeded with excellent regio and diastereoselectivity to give the allylic alcohol in 90% yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1). Subsequent deprotection of the TIPS group gave diol 32 in 90% yield.

evidence.text_span: Luche reduction (NaBH4, CeCl3·7H2O, EtOH, −78 to −60 °C)32 of 29-(Z) proceeded with excellent regio and diastereoselectivity to give the allylic alcohol in 90% yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1). The configuration of the newly generated stereogenic center at C-15 from ketone 29-(Z) by Luche reduction was established by a modified Mosher analysis (see Supporting Information).22 Subsequent deprotection of the TIPS group gave diol 32 in 90% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Challenges of Transannular Acetal Formation and Construction of the Macrocyclic Acetal Skeleton of Portimine

thought.background: Transannular acetal formation from diol 32 is expected to be more favorable than from diol 30.
thought.gap: Need to form the transannular acetal from diol 32.
thought.decision: To obtain acetal 33, chose transannular acetal formation of 32 by the action of TsOH·H2O in CH2Cl2, expecting smooth reaction and good yield.

outcome.observation: To our delight, transannular acetal formation of 32 proceeded smoothly by the action of TsOH·H2O in CH2Cl2 to afford acetal 33 in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: To our delight, transannular acetal formation of 32 proceeded smoothly by the action of TsOH·H2O in CH2Cl2 to afford acetal 33 in 91% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Challenges of Transannular Acetal Formation and Construction of the Macrocyclic Acetal Skeleton of Portimine

thought.background: The thiocarbamate group needs to be removed and the formyl group removed to obtain the final core skeleton.
thought.gap: Need to convert acetal 33 to alcohol 35, which has the same core skeleton as portimine.
thought.decision: To synthesize alcohol 35, chose selective removal of the thiocarbamate group from 33 by exposure to m-CPBA to provide formyl ester 34, followed by removal of the formyl group, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: Selective removal of the thiocarbamate group by exposure of 33 to m-CPBA provided formyl ester 34. Finally, alcohol 35, with the same core skeleton as portimine, was synthesized by removal of the formyl group in 63% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Selective removal of the thiocarbamate group28 by exposure of 33 to m-CPBA provided formyl ester 34. Finally, alcohol 35, with the same core skeleton as portimine, was synthesized by removal of the formyl group in 63% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Challenges of Transannular Acetal Formation and Construction of the Macrocyclic Acetal Skeleton of Portimine

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the synthesis of the portimine core skeleton (35) was not explicitly stated, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Number of steps in the longest linear sequence to reach the core skeleton 35.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of distinct synthetic operations reported.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.significance: The synthesis successfully constructed the core skeleton of portimine (35) using an organocatalyst-mediated asymmetric stannylation and a stereoretentive copper-catalyzed C(sp3)-C(sp2) Stille-type cross-coupling, which are highlighted as key methodologies. The RCM and transannular acetal formation steps were also crucial for macrocycle construction. This work provides a foundation for the total synthesis of portimine.

source.title: Stereocontrolled Synthesis of the Portimine Skeleton via Organocatalyst-Mediated Asymmetric Stannylation and Stereoretentive C(sp3)-C(sp2) Stille Coupling.
source.authors: Sato D, Sasaki M, Umehara A.
